walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Geisha Cards
walkthrough.text: Let's set up Hanamikoji! Place the seven Geisha cards in a row between the two players, from lowest charm value to highest. Each Geisha shows a charm value of two, two, two, three, three, four, or five. Place a victory marker on each Geisha card in the neutral center position.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Item Deck
walkthrough.text: Shuffle all twenty-one item cards together. These cards come in seven types matching the seven Geisha. The number of cards per type matches that Geisha's charm value. Randomly remove one card from the deck face down without looking at it. This creates uncertainty about what is available.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Hands
walkthrough.text: Deal six cards to each player from the remaining deck. The eight cards left over form the draw pile. Place it face down between the players. Each player also takes their four action tokens and places them face up in front of themselves.

walkthrough.title: Choose First Player
walkthrough.text: Pick a first player randomly. In Hanamikoji, players alternate turns, drawing a card and performing one action each turn. Each player will take exactly four turns per round, using each action token exactly once.

summary.title: Lay Out the Geisha Cards
bullets: Place 7 Geisha cards in a row between players
Charm values: 2, 2, 2, 3, 3, 4, 5
Place victory markers in neutral position

summary.title: Prepare the Item Deck
bullets: Shuffle 21 item cards
Remove 1 card face down (hidden information)
7 types matching the 7 Geisha

summary.title: Deal Starting Hands
bullets: Deal 6 cards to each player
Remaining 8 cards = draw pile
Each player takes 4 action tokens

summary.title: Choose First Player
bullets: Random selection
Players alternate turns, 4 turns each per round
Each action token used exactly once

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you do two things in order. First, draw one card from the draw pile and add it to your hand. Second, choose one of your four unused action tokens and perform that action. Each token can only be used once per round, so after eight total turns the round ends.

walkthrough.title: Action: Secret
walkthrough.text: The Secret action lets you place one card from your hand face down in front of you. Nobody sees what it is until the end of the round. At that point, the card is revealed and placed on your side of the matching Geisha. This is your chance to guarantee one card goes exactly where you want it.

walkthrough.title: Action: Trade
walkthrough.text: The Trade action removes two cards from the game entirely. Place two cards from your hand face down. These are set aside and neither player gets them. They are gone for the round. This is powerful for eliminating cards your opponent needs.

walkthrough.title: Action: Gift
walkthrough.text: The Gift action forces a painful choice. Place three cards from your hand face up. Your opponent chooses one of the three to take. The remaining two go to your side of their matching Geisha. You get two cards, but your opponent picks the best one for themselves.

walkthrough.title: Action: Competition
walkthrough.text: The Competition action is the biggest dilemma. Place four cards from your hand face up and arrange them into two pairs. Your opponent chooses one pair to take. You get the other pair. Both pairs go to the respective players' sides of the matching Geisha. Pair construction is everything here.

walkthrough.title: Winning Geisha Favor
walkthrough.text: At the end of the round, compare item cards on each side of every Geisha. The player with more cards of that Geisha's type wins her favor and moves the marker to their side. If tied, the marker stays where it is. Favor carries over between rounds.

walkthrough.title: Victory Conditions
walkthrough.text: Check two win conditions after each round. If one player has the favor of four or more Geisha, they win. Alternatively, if one player has Geisha totaling eleven or more charm points, they win. If neither condition is met, play another round with Geisha favor carrying over.

walkthrough.title: Starting a New Round
walkthrough.text: If nobody has won, start a new round. Collect all item cards, shuffle them, remove one face down, deal six to each player, and reset the draw pile. Crucially, Geisha markers stay where they are. You only need to flip favor, not rebuild it from scratch. Most games end in one to three rounds.

Perform 1 of 4 unused actions
Each action used exactly once per round

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Geisha District
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Hanamikoji! This is a two-player game of painful choices and bluffing. We will walk through a practice round where you will see each of the four actions in use and learn from two common beginner mistakes. Let's deal out our cards and begin.

walkthrough.title: Reading Your Starting Hand
walkthrough.text: Look at your six starting cards. Identify which Geisha types you have the most of. If you have two cards for the five-charm Geisha, that is very strong. If you have a spread of single cards across many types, you will need to be more strategic about which Geisha to fight for.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Turn: Secret
walkthrough.text: Player 1 draws a card, bringing their hand to seven. They decide to use the Secret action first, placing one card face down. This is smart because the Secret action guarantees a card goes to your side. Player 1 hides their best card for the five-charm Geisha, ensuring it scores for them no matter what.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Turn: Competition
walkthrough.text: Player 2 draws a card and uses the Competition action. They place four cards face up in two pairs. Player 2 tries to construct two pairs where both are roughly equal in value, making the choice hard for Player 1. Player 1 studies both pairs carefully and takes the one that helps their Geisha targets most.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Mistake: Bad Gift
walkthrough.text: Player 1 draws a card and uses the Gift action. They place three cards face up, but one of them is a card for the five-charm Geisha. Player 2 immediately takes it. Player 1 just gave away a critical card for the most valuable Geisha in the game. This could cost them the five-charm Geisha entirely.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Never Gift What You Need
walkthrough.text: When using the Gift action, never include a card you desperately want to keep. Your opponent will always pick the most valuable card for themselves. The Gift should contain three cards that are all acceptable for you to lose. Ideally, offer cards from Geisha you have already secured or have given up on.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Turn: Trade
walkthrough.text: Player 2 draws and uses the Trade action to remove two cards from the game. They trade away two cards that would have helped Player 1 compete for the three-charm Geisha. Smart play. The Trade action is great for denying your opponent resources.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Turn: Competition
walkthrough.text: Player 1 draws and uses Competition, placing four cards in two pairs. They put their two weakest cards together as one pair and two decent cards as the other. Player 2 naturally takes the better pair, but Player 1 is fine losing those because they kept what they needed.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Mistake: Wasting the Secret
walkthrough.text: Player 2 draws and uses the Secret action to hide a card for a two-charm Geisha. But Player 2 already has enough cards on their side to win that Geisha even without the secret card. They just wasted their most powerful action on a Geisha they already controlled.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Use Secret on Contested Geisha
walkthrough.text: The Secret action is your only guaranteed placement. Use it on a Geisha where the contest is tight, not on one you have already won. The Secret card should be the tipping point that flips a contested Geisha in your favor. Wasting it on a safe Geisha is like spending your best move on a battle already won.

walkthrough.title: Remaining Turns
walkthrough.text: Both players use their remaining actions. Player 2 uses Gift, carefully offering three low-value cards. Player 1 uses Trade to eliminate two cards that would have helped Player 2. With all eight turns complete, reveal the Secret cards and compare each Geisha.

walkthrough.title: Scoring the Round
walkthrough.text: Count the cards on each side of every Geisha. Move markers to reflect the winner of each. Then check the two win conditions. Does either player have four Geisha? Does either player have eleven charm points worth of Geisha? If so, they win. If not, the markers stay and you play another round.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations! You have seen all four actions in Hanamikoji. You learned that the Gift should never include critical cards, and that the Secret should target contested Geisha, not safe ones. This game is all about forcing painful decisions on your opponent while protecting what matters most to you.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin
walkthrough.text:
Before you start a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to actions and game terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Begin
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics
Reference the Appendix → actions and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for answers

walkthrough.title: Action Timing Strategy
walkthrough.text: The order you use your four actions matters enormously. Using Competition early when you have more cards gives you better pair construction options. Using Secret early protects your plan before your opponent has information. Using Gift late when you have fewer cards limits what you can offer. Think about action timing as the core strategic layer of Hanamikoji.

walkthrough.title: Focus on the Five-Charm Geisha
walkthrough.text: The five-charm Geisha is worth almost half of the eleven needed to win by charm alone. There are five item cards for her, meaning you need three to win her favor. Fight hard for this Geisha because controlling her makes it much easier to reach eleven charm.

walkthrough.title: The Missing Card Matters
walkthrough.text: One card is removed at setup. You do not know which one. This uncertainty is critical for the two-charm Geisha that only have two cards total. If the missing card is one of those types, the single remaining card wins that Geisha. Factor this uncertainty into every decision.

walkthrough.title: Gift Poison Strategy
walkthrough.text: The best Gifts contain three cards that are all somewhat useful to you. Your opponent must pick the least bad option for you. Ideally, all three cards belong to Geisha you are winning or do not care about. Forcing your opponent to choose between three mediocre options is the Gift's true power.

walkthrough.title: Competition Pair Construction
walkthrough.text: When constructing pairs for Competition, make one pair clearly stronger than the other so your opponent takes the obvious choice, leaving you with a pair that subtly advances your actual plan. Alternatively, make both pairs equally painful to give away so your opponent has no clear right answer.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Three common beginner mistakes will cost you games. First, including critical cards in your Gift offer. Second, wasting the Secret on a Geisha you already control. Third, ignoring what your opponent's remaining actions are. If they have not used their Trade yet, they could still eliminate two of your key cards.

walkthrough.title: Reading Your Opponent
walkthrough.text: Track which actions your opponent has used. If they still have Secret available, they are probably holding a key card. If they have used Trade, those eliminated cards tell you what Geisha they are not fighting for. Every action your opponent takes gives you information about their strategy.

summary.title: Action Timing Strategy
bullets: Competition early (more cards = better pairs)
Secret early (protect plan before opponent knows)
Gift late (fewer cards = fewer options to give)

summary.title: Focus on the Five-Charm Geisha

Controlling her makes 11-charm win much easier

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't gift critical cards
Don't waste Secret on safe Geisha
Don't ignore opponent's remaining actions

summary.title: Reading Your Opponent
bullets: Track which actions opponent has used
Secret still available = they're holding a key card
Traded cards reveal which Geisha they've abandoned

walkthrough.title: Four Geisha vs Eleven Charm
walkthrough.text: There are two paths to victory. Winning four of seven Geisha is easier if you focus on the three two-charm Geisha plus one three-charm Geisha. Going for eleven charm means winning the five-charm Geisha and at least one four-charm or two three-charm Geisha. Decide your path early and build your action plan around it.

walkthrough.title: Trade as Denial
walkthrough.text: The Trade action is most powerful when you eliminate cards your opponent desperately needs. If you see your opponent fighting for the four-charm Geisha, trading away those cards can swing the contest. The best Trade targets are cards for Geisha where your opponent is one card away from winning favor.

walkthrough.title: Multi-Round Planning
walkthrough.text: In a multi-round game, Geisha favor carries over. This means winning a Geisha by a large margin is wasted effort. Winning by a single card is just as good. Save your resources for contested Geisha in future rounds. A slim victory now is worth more than a blowout because the marker only needs to be on your side.

walkthrough.title: Deception Through Action Order
walkthrough.text: Your action order reveals information. Using Secret first tells your opponent you are protecting something important. Using Competition first can be a bluff, suggesting you have strong cards when you might actually be dumping weak ones. Vary your action order between games to stay unpredictable.

walkthrough.title: Card Counting
walkthrough.text: There are twenty-one cards in specific quantities per Geisha type. With one removed at setup, twenty remain. As cards appear through actions, track what has been played. If all five cards of the five-charm Geisha have been accounted for, the contest for that Geisha is settled. Card counting lets you make confident decisions late in the round.

walkthrough.title: Forcing Opponent Actions
walkthrough.text: If your opponent has only one action left, you know exactly what they will do. This lets you plan your final action with perfect information. Try to be the player who uses their last action with more knowledge. Going second in the round can be advantageous because you see your opponent's final move before yours.

walkthrough.title: The Two-Charm Geisha Gambit
walkthrough.text: The three two-charm Geisha each have only two item cards. Winning even one of these cards guarantees at minimum a tie. If the removed card happens to be that Geisha's type, a single card wins outright. Focusing on winning all three two-charm Geisha is a viable path to four Geisha victory that many opponents overlook.

summary.title: Four Geisha vs Eleven Charm
bullets: 4 Geisha path: target small Geisha (2+2+2+3)
11 charm path: must win the 5-charm + another large one
Decide path early and build plan around it

summary.title: Trade as Denial
bullets: Eliminate cards your opponent needs for contested Geisha
Best targets: cards where opponent is 1 away from winning

summary.title: Multi-Round Planning
bullets: Favor carries over — winning by 1 card is enough
Don't over-invest in Geisha you already control
Slim wins preserve resources for future rounds

summary.title: Deception Through Action Order
bullets: Action order reveals information — vary it
Early Secret suggests protecting something important
Early Competition can be a bluff

summary.title: Card Counting
bullets: 21 cards minus 1 removed = 20 in play
Track what's been played through all actions
Settled contests let you focus elsewhere

summary.title: Forcing Opponent Actions
bullets: Last action with 1 choice = perfect information for you
Going second can be advantageous
Try to have more info when making your final play

summary.title: The Two-Charm Geisha Gambit
bullets: 2-charm Geisha have only 2 cards each
1 card = guaranteed tie or win (if other was removed)
Sweeping all three small Geisha = viable 4-Geisha path

entries.definition: Action that places one card face down, revealed at round end and added to your side of the matching Geisha. Guaranteed uncontested placement.
Action that places two cards face down and removes them from the game. Neither player gets them. Used to deny opponent key cards.
Action that places three cards face up. Opponent picks one for themselves, you get the remaining two on your side of matching Geisha.
Action that places four cards face up in two pairs you construct. Opponent picks one pair, you get the other. Both pairs go to respective sides of matching Geisha.
One of seven cards placed between players representing the Geisha whose favor you are competing for. Each has a charm value from two to five.
The number printed on a Geisha card, ranging from two to five. Reaching eleven total charm from Geisha in your favor is one win condition.
Token placed on each Geisha card showing which player has her favor. Starts neutral. Moves to winner's side after each round.
One of twenty-one cards in seven types matching the seven Geisha. The number of cards per type equals that Geisha's charm value.
Winning a Geisha by having more item cards on your side than your opponent. Favor persists between rounds unless the opponent flips it.
One random item card removed face down during setup. Creates hidden information and uncertainty about card distribution.
Win condition: gain favor of four or more Geisha at the end of a round, regardless of their charm values.
Win condition: control Geisha whose charm values total eleven or more at the end of a round.
Geisha markers carry over between rounds. You only need to change the contest outcome, not rebuild from scratch.
Each player has four tokens representing the four actions. Each must be used exactly once per round, in any order the player chooses.
The eight remaining item cards after dealing and removing one. Players draw one card per turn, expanding their hand before choosing an action.
